specific_location: Big Sandy Creek at County Road 125 at Simla
description: this bird was foraging on an extensive mat of vegetation debris (leaves, wood bits, etc) caught by some tree trunks in the recently flooded stream bed, where it was joined by a Swainson's Thrush, several Yellow-rumped Warblers, and Common Grackles. It was about 50 feet away, and when I first viewed it through binoculars I observed features that indicated it was not one of the more common thrushes. So after watching it for a short time I went to get my spotting scope from the car. It kept coming back to the same mat of debris and I had great views through the scope as it fed on the mat and perched low in the adjacent trees. The skies were mostly clear and the bird was sometimes in sunlight and otherwise in bright shade. Views were mostly unobstructed when it was on the debris mat or when it perched low in the trees beside the debris. I was able to view it for about 6-7 minutes over a period of about 18 minutes, occasionally being distracted by the many other birds in the area. My initial views that made it appear different compared to the adjacent SWTH were of a less warm-colored bird, with darker spotting on the breast without buff tones and a lack of a noticeable eyering. The bird showed a gray face that lacked warm tones, especially noticeable in the loral and malar areas and toward the rear of the auriculars. Compared to the SWTH the auriculars were grayish and not warm brown. The eyering was weak and not at all pronounced. In my binocular views I did not really notice it, but in the scope it was weakly evident, and showed as whitish or pale and not buff-pale as on SWTH. The difference with the obvious eyering of the nearby SWTH was clearly evident. The breast was well marked with blackish spots, the spots being somewhat darker and more distinct than on the SWTH. The base color of the breast and sides was cool pale grayish in tone and lacked the buffy suffusion of SWTH. The upperparts were gray-brown, with the tail being just a bit more brown (less gray) so there was weak contrast with the back. The flanks had a pale grayish wash nicely evident through the scope when the bird was in the bright shade, lacking buff or warm coloring. The bird appeared the same size as SWTH. The legs were flesh. The bill appeared similar to SWTH in coloration and shape. I did not notice much tail or wing flicking. I did not hear any calls from this bird, but with the stream flow it would have been hard to hear any that may have been given.
